channel.title: Posts on pretentiousUsername
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on pretentiousUsername

item.title: The hummingbird: a synth I'm working on
item.description: When people think of analog synthesizers, they—and I—like to think of massive, hulking modular systems, steel Junos, and woodgrain. Of course, that’s all great—I love that kind of stuff. But, here’s a problem: What if I wanted to make something small enough to fit in a backpack while remaining affordable?—and what I wanted to give it features that are out of the ordinary for an average synthesizer? That’s the main goal of the Hummingbird—something that is as small as possible while not sacrificing on features.
item.content:
When people think of analog synthesizers, they—and I—like to think of massive, hulking modular systems, steel Junos, and woodgrain. Of course, that’s all great—I love that kind of stuff. But, here’s a problem: What if I wanted to make something small enough to fit in a backpack while remaining affordable?—and what I wanted to give it features that are out of the ordinary for an average synthesizer?

That’s the main goal of the Hummingbird—something that is as small as possible while not sacrificing on features. The main goal is to offer a synthesizer that has stable tuning, small size, and a massive sound. But I’m not satisfied with taking the easy route—such as throwing in a bunch of LM13700s1—I want to make something more distinct. For starters, I’m planning on having the CV generated by a Raspberry Pi Pico and a DAC,2 which would allow for not only Western 12-note tonality, but allow for user-programmed tuning systems.

The inside of the Hummingbird will be composed of module cards which will allow for easy replacement of components, and a smooth build process. This is due to the fact that I’m trying to make almost every single component in this synthesizer, due to the size constraint, surface-mount (SMD). While through-hole is superior for soldering—no one likes having to solder tiny resistors—size constraints would make their liberal use a daunting task. On the flipside, SMD parts have a lower thermal mass, generate less noise, and are generally cheaper than through-hole components. So yeah, those module cards will be absolutely necessary for any DIYer that wants to build the synth, but doesn’t have the money for a microscope.

The VCO section will be based off of René Schmitz’s CMOS VCO, which I find interesting due to the fact it’s using a CD4069 to make a complete VCO. I’ll be adding in my own twist—a flip-flop based suboscillator, and a “superoscillator” that will be one octave above the VCO. This will be so I can squeeze out as much sound as possible, even with a single integrated circuit—I also love the sound of the SH-101’s suboscillator.3

Other than that, I’m planning on going a bit big and throwing either a Moog derivative filter in there, or a discrete recreation of the SSM2044 VCF.4 A synth is 75% filter, and 25% other stuff, after all.5

That’s basically everything I have so far. I hope to get this synth done within a year. I have a substantial amount of theoretical work done, I just need to actually build this thing, design the PCBs, and then design a case to put it all in. Once I’m done, I might send one to my old electronics teacher from high school—since I spent about half of my time in that classroom either reading about synths, building synth circuits, or reading about physics stuff.

  1. To be honest, I think that the LM13700 is one of the most overused components in synthesizer history. It is useful, and quite good, but virtually every synthesizer from here to Timbuktu uses it. ↩︎

  2. I’ll likely use a TI dac60501 due to its precision internal reference voltage, and its affordable price. ↩︎

  3. One particular advantage the CD4069 has over a traditional synthesizer VCO chip, such as the SSI2130, is its combination of excellent thermal stability (see figure 2 of the CD4069 datasheet), wide availability, and cheap price. ↩︎

  4. See Rossum, David. 1983-09-13. Lowpass filter with electronic control of cutoff and resonance. US Patent 4,404,529, filed 1980-12-30, and issued 1983-09-13. ↩︎

  5. Another interesting filter is shown in patent DE20 2010 016 326 U1 by Thomas Strauss, which uses some transistors, diodes, and an op amp to make a filter. I’m thinking of having multiple options, and making the pins for both filters’ modules identical. ↩︎

item.title: Anime review: *Keep your hands off Eizouken!*
item.description: Honestly, I don’t think any other show will come close to Keep your hands off Eizouken!, and not to their own detriment. Eizouken is a show unlike any other that I’ve seen—it’s bold, imaginative, and incredibly well animated, all while keeping a story with relatively few stakes, and only three main characters. The show’s plot is somewhat self-explanatory—three high school girls make a club to produce anime, hijinks ensue. In many ways, the plot is low-stakes—characters are never in any serious danger;1 and the only violence in the show is both for comedic effect, and never ends up hurting anyone.
item.content:
Honestly, I don’t think any other show will come close to Keep your hands off Eizouken!, and not to their own detriment. Eizouken is a show unlike any other that I’ve seen—it’s bold, imaginative, and incredibly well animated, all while keeping a story with relatively few stakes, and only three main characters.

The show’s plot is somewhat self-explanatory—three high school girls make a club to produce anime, hijinks ensue. In many ways, the plot is low-stakes—characters are never in any serious danger;1 and the only violence in the show is both for comedic effect, and never ends up hurting anyone. (If you’re a parent with a kid in elementary school, and you’re cool with some mild language, this show would be perfect for your kid.)

To be honest, Eizouken is two shows in one, first, it is an anime, and second, it is an explanation of the anime industry. Each of the three main characters do fulfill a main roll in the industry: concept art/direction, animation, and business management.2 The show constantly manages to weave in ways to explain how anime is made; Eizouken manages to be educational without ever feeling that way, which is no mean feat at all.

Indeed, Eizouken is fantastically playful, and never dry. Peppered in are sections of characters coming up with new ideas and machines to put into their show, lovingly animated, and placed into the world as though they were real. Play itself is emphasized in the show quite heavily—despite being in high school, all of the characters never really let go of the hobbies that inspired them. I find that to be a pretty major theme in the show: Characters are mature, and capable of knowing what, and why they love what to do, but still manage to keep the awe and wonder that inspired them in the first place.

Eizouken’s animation is fantastic—dare I say it is damn near perfect. Oftentimes, Eizouken actually has a much higher framerate than expected from most anime, all while still mostly using—presumably—hand-drawn art and animation. (Of course, Science Saru worked on this show—so of course animation would be top-notch.) My personal highlights are the sections where the main three—and any other characters with them—brainstorm machines and ideas to put into whatever anime they’re working on. Almost every single physical detail was painstakingly taken into account.

Exceeding the animation is just the world the characters live in. Most buildings in the show are massive, sprawling complexes; there is a definite urbanism to Eizouken—every area is pedestrian friendly, public transit is everywhere, and dense, mixed-use housing is everywhere. That said, within the world, nature is still everywhere; the urban and “natural” world are not portrayed as in constant struggle between one-another, but as, in a sense, symbiotic.

Moreover, despite a few quirks added in for comedy’s sake—like an ultra-strict school council, riot cops in a school, and organized “crime” between school clubs—Eizouken shows a world that, despite not being a utopia, is far from a dystopia. Characters are diverse, and little hints throughout the world show how, for instance, there are multiple languages used within the school; that people are actively trying to make the world easier for disabled people (see 08:10 in episode 8); and that Japan has become, for the most part, racially integrated.3 Even if Eizouken’s world is not a utopia, it is far from dystopia, and in many ways presents a future that would be far preferable to today.

All in all, if I had to describe Eizouken in one word, it would be unique. Very few shows manage to do almost everything right, and by god Eizouken is one of them. If you have about half an hour of free time, put an episode on—and then free up four more hours to watch the rest.

Plot: 8/10

Animation: 10/10

World: 10/10

Pacing: 7/10

Dialogue: 8/10

Overall rating: 9/10

  1. They were once, but it was quickly ended by a few characters bringing a rope to traverse pits. ↩︎

  2. A later character handles sound design. ↩︎

  3. One huge improvement Eizouken made over made over an unfortunate amount of other anime is how its Black characters aren’t drawn as racist caricatures. ↩︎

item.title: Testing out some stuff with markdown
item.description: The Lindblad master equation is a Markovian, first-order differential equation for calculating the time-evolution of a density matrix, \(\rho\). $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\frac{i}{\hbar} \left[ H, \rho \right] + \sum_{i = 1}^{N^2 - 1} \gamma_i \left( L_i \rho L_i^\dagger - \frac{1}{2} \overline{ \left[ L_i^\dagger L_i, \rho \right] } \right) , $$ where \( \overline{[A, B]} \) is the anticommutator.1 This is a table Table of tables foo fooo foooo Unfortunately, for whatever reason, KaTeX doesn’t like using escaped \left\{ and \right\}—so much so that they’re not rendered into the equation—therefore, I must make due.
item.content:
The Lindblad master equation is a Markovian, first-order differential equation for calculating the time-evolution of a density matrix, \(\rho\).

$$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\frac{i}{\hbar} \left[ H, \rho \right] + \sum_{i = 1}^{N^2 - 1} \gamma_i \left( L_i \rho L_i^\dagger - \frac{1}{2} \overline{ \left[ L_i^\dagger L_i, \rho \right] } \right) , $$ where \( \overline{[A, B]} \) is the anticommutator.1

This is a table

Table of tables
foo fooo foooo

  1. Unfortunately, for whatever reason, KaTeX doesn’t like using escaped \left\{ and \right\}—so much so that they’re not rendered into the equation—therefore, I must make due. ↩︎

item.title: In the begining
item.description: Yeah—that’s a real imaginative title. No one else has ever ripped of the Bible. Behold! Anyways, this is my new blog. I’m pretty sure absolutely no one in their right mind would want to read it, but I like writing, and I figured it’d be nice to put it all in one place. Many people, as far as I know, like to keep blogs for employability’s sake—write about something in programming or whatnot—but for me, I just need a place to do some long-form writing.
item.content:
Yeah—that’s a real imaginative title. No one else has ever ripped of the Bible.

Behold!

Anyways, this is my new blog. I’m pretty sure absolutely no one in their right mind would want to read it, but I like writing, and I figured it’d be nice to put it all in one place.

Many people, as far as I know, like to keep blogs for employability’s sake—write about something in programming or whatnot—but for me, I just need a place to do some long-form writing. Social media sucks—I certainly wouldn’t want to use a site like Facebook for writing—so a Hugo blog it is.

Hugo isn’t very good

Not saying I could do better, but Hugo isn’t very good—or, at the very least, there aren’t many good themes for it; most static site generators aren’t very good.

Do you know how hard I hunted to find a new static site generator? I spent a whole hour trying to find one, and ended up with Hugo. It was either Hugo, dealing with javascript,1 or using something ancient.

Perhaps I simply do not understand how static site generators work, but I find working on Hugo stuff to be mind-boggling. So many themes tend to feel overcomplicated and almost impossible to edit well—even the erroneously named “shortcodes” people implement tend to be much longer, and much more easy to forget, than Markdown syntax.

That said, it’s still faster than the alternatives, and I don’t need to use javascript—that’s a plus, and likely the only reason I’m using Hugo.

All that said, I can’t complain much; Hugo is much more customizable than something like Squarespace or something like that, my site is hosted for free on Github—and, best of all, it’s not WordPress.

  1. You may also know javascript as the worst programming language known to humankind. ↩︎
